findings.claim: The canonical Ralph loop's hardest rule is 'one item per loop': each iteration gets a fresh context, reads state from disk (PROMPT.md, fix_plan.md, AGENT.md, specs, git history), does exactly one task, and commits. Huntley repeats it verbatim: 'One item per loop. I need to repeat myself here—one item per loop.' He also advises 'deterministically allocate the stack the same way every loop' (always load the same specs/plan files even if wasteful) and to periodically delete and regenerate the plan file ('throw it out often') to kill stale-plan drift.
findings.source: https://ghuntley.com/ralph/ (Geoffrey Huntley, 'The Ralph Wiggum Loop, from first principles')
findings.relevance: Our 'one-feature-per-builder conservatism' is not an inefficiency — it is the strongest cross-source consensus pattern; the transferable deltas are deterministic context loading for builders and periodically regenerating CAMPAIGN.md from the ledger instead of letting it accrete.

findings.claim: Ralph's economics depend on 'backpressure': cheap deterministic rejection gates (compiler errors, unit tests, type checkers, pluggable validators) that reject bad work before any expensive review. Huntley frames loop throughput as 'the speed of the wheel turning, balanced against the axis of correctness' — the loop only pays off when validation is automatic.
findings.source: https://ghuntley.com/ralph/ plus HN consensus at https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46672413 ('if the feature can't be autovalidated, Ralph shouldn't be used')
findings.relevance: Our only deterministic gate is the boot-gate; everything else (regressions, feature verification) is validated by expensive agents — building deterministic backpressure from the existing window.__vox hooks is the single highest-leverage change to our 50-70%-of-cost rating phase.

findings.claim: Anthropic's 'Effective harnesses for long-running agents' splits the run into a one-time initializer agent and per-session coding agents, with durable artifacts: a feature-list JSON of 200+ granular features each carrying a `passes` boolean, a claude-progress.txt log, an init.sh boot script, and the hard prompt rule 'It is unacceptable to remove or edit tests.' End-to-end browser verification (Puppeteer) 'dramatically improved performance' over code-only review.
findings.relevance: A persistent feature ledger with machine-checkable pass booleans converts our biggest delta source ('verification conversion' of built-but-unverified features) from an expensive per-round agent probe into a cheap incremental re-check, and gives the regression hunter a precise diff to audit.

findings.claim: Anthropic's C-compiler project ran 16 parallel Claude agents on ONE shared repo for two weeks (~2,000 sessions, 2B input / 140M output tokens, ~$20K) using only naive coordination: lock files in current_tasks/ claimed via git ('if two agents try to claim the same task, git's synchronization forces the second agent to pick a different one'), local clones merged back to upstream, merge conflicts left to Claude ('Claude is smart enough to figure that out'). Regressions were tamed mid-run by adding a CI pipeline and an online known-good oracle (GCC) to compare against.
findings.relevance: Parallel edits to one artifact are demonstrably workable with worktrees + lock files + model-mediated merges, so our serial-builder constraint is a choice, not a law — and the GCC-oracle trick maps to keeping a frozen known-good build to diff behavior against for cheap regression detection.

findings.claim: Carlini's test-harness design lessons: test output must be terse ('should not print thousands of useless bytes'; put ERROR and reason on one greppable line), provide a --fast mode running a 1%/10% random sample ('deterministic per-agent but random across VMs') because 'Claude can't tell time and, left alone, will happily spend hours running tests instead of making progress,' and pre-compute summaries so Claude doesn't recompute them. His 14:1 input:output token ratio shows reading/probing dominates cost.
findings.relevance: Maps directly onto our rating redundancy: a sampled fast-tier check every round (touched features + ~10% random regression sample) with the full 5-seat panel reserved for milestones, plus pre-computed probe summaries, attacks the dominant input-token cost.

findings.claim: Practitioner overnight-run architecture (Khmelinskaya): single long sessions die of silent context exhaustion and compaction causes 'instruction dilution'; the working fix is phased fresh `claude --print` sessions chained through a STATUS.md handoff file, a CLAUDE.md rule to redirect all command output to files then `tail -20` (estimated ~10x context budget), per-phase `--max-budget-usd` caps, and a /goal-style completion condition evaluated by a fast cheap model after each turn instead of an external watchdog. Result: 'All 5 phases completed successfully. Zero failures.'
findings.relevance: Our round structure already does fresh sessions, but the output-to-file-then-tail discipline for probe/sim logs and a fast-model completion-condition check before invoking expensive judges are cheap drop-in token savers.

findings.claim: Anthropic's multi-agent research system data: multi-agent setups burn ~15x the tokens of single chats, token usage alone explains ~80% of performance variance on BrowseComp, and the key prompt-engineering fix was explicit effort-scaling rules — the lead agent is told how many subagents and tool calls each query class deserves, because agents 'struggled to judge appropriate resource allocation,' over- or under-spending without it.
findings.relevance: Argues for replacing our fixed 3-lane rounds with planner-side effort-scaling rules (projected_next_gain -> lane count and rating tier), and warns that adding more judge/hunter agents is the cost direction to avoid — substitute deterministic checks instead.

findings.claim: HN skepticism threads converge on where bare loops fail: 'no verification gates, weak recovery, context pollution across steps, and expensive runaway loops' for structured multi-phase deliverables; the fix practitioners report is 'isolating steps and passing explicit state/artifacts between them, with verification + guardrails,' plus iteration/time caps so the loop can't burn the budget. Naive same-prompt-forever Ralph costs 'hundreds of dollars per workflow — not because the idea is bad, but because the workflow shape is.'
findings.relevance: Validates our planner+ledger shape over naive looping (our deltas track deliberately opened family weight, exactly the structured case bare Ralph fails), and flags our re-done exploratory probing as the classic 'context pollution / re-exploration' tax to eliminate via persisted probe artifacts.

findings.claim: Cross-practitioner aggregation (Osmani): the test ratchet ('unacceptable to remove or edit tests'), planner/worker/judge role separation where judges decide completion (Cursor found 'equal-status agents writing to shared files' failed and that model choice per role matters — a GPT model beat Opus for extended autonomous work because Opus stopped early), and the blunt assessment that cost control is 'genuinely unsolved' beyond budgets and circuit breakers.
findings.relevance: Confirms our judge-separate-from-builder design is right; the actionable delta is a test ratchet rule in builder prompts (never weaken a __vox check to make a feature pass) and per-phase budget circuit breakers since nobody has a smarter cost-control trick to copy.

recommendations: Build the regression ledger as deterministic backpressure: an initializer-style pass converts every feature CAMPAIGN.md considers 'verified' into a feature-list JSON entry with a `passes` boolean backed by a headless window.__vox check; run the full suite at boot-gate each round for near-zero agent tokens. The adversarial regression hunter then audits only this round's diff plus suite gaps, and runs full-breadth only every Nth round. This attacks the 50-70% rating share directly (Anthropic harness post + Carlini CI lesson).

Tier verification Carlini-style: per-round fast tier = features touched this round + a ~10% random sample of the ledger (random across rounds, deterministic within one); full 5-seat panel only every 3rd round or when the delta judge's score disagrees with projection beyond a threshold. Also re-score a frozen reference build occasionally as a known-good oracle to detect judge drift, since incremental judging can wander.

Stop re-deriving the world: persist a probe map (PROBE_STATE.json / PROBE_MAP.md with feature -> exact reproduction steps, API calls, expected observations) that the live probe agent reads first; it verifies only deltas plus the random sample. Pair with output discipline rules in probe/builder prompts (sim logs to file, tail summaries only) — the practitioner-estimated ~10x context saving lands mostly in our probe phase, and Carlini's 14:1 input:output ratio says reading is where our tokens go.

Replace fixed 3 lanes with planner-side effort scaling: CAMPAIGN.md's projected_next_gain decides lanes (1-4) and rating tier per round, with explicit rules in the planner prompt (Anthropic research-system finding that agents misallocate effort without such rules). Keep one-feature-per-builder — every credible source independently converged on it; do not 'fix' that.

Parallelize builders only if wall-time ever becomes binding, not for token efficiency: the single-file constraint is on the artifact, so either split the HTML into tree modules concatenated by a build step, or use Carlini's worktrees + current_tasks/ lock files with one merge agent. Evidence says it works (16 agents, naive locking, model-mediated merges), but it adds merge-agent tokens — since our binding resource is tokens, this is lower priority than the rating-cost cuts and should be adjudicated as such.

Add two cheap guardrails from the overnight-run playbook: a fast-model /goal-style completion condition that checks each builder's acceptance criterion (feature observable via __vox, boot clean) before any expensive judge runs, and per-phase budget caps with a circuit breaker — plus a test-ratchet line in builder prompts forbidding weakening or deleting __vox checks to make a feature pass.
reports.summary: Practitioner evidence on long-running agentic loops (Ralph Wiggum corpus, Anthropic's long-running-harness and C-compiler posts, overnight-run writeups, HN postmortems) converges on a small set of mechanisms: fresh context per task with state on disk, one item per loop, a persistent machine-checkable feature ledger with pass booleans, terse greppable test harnesses with a sampled fast tier, test ratchets, planner/worker/judge separation, and explicit effort-scaling rules because agents misallocate resources by default. Adjudicated against our loop: our shape (planner + ledger + serial one-feature builders + separate judges) already matches best practice — the real inefficiencies are paying expensive agents to do what deterministic checks could do. The highest-leverage moves are (1) a __vox-backed regression suite run at boot-gate so verification conversion and regression hunting become near-free, (2) tiered, sampled rating with a frozen-build oracle against judge drift, (3) a persisted probe map ending per-round re-exploration, and (4) variable lane count via planner effort rules. Parallel builders are proven feasible (16 Claudes, one repo, lock files) but spend tokens to save wall-time, the wrong trade for our token-bound loop; and one-feature-per-builder should be kept, not fixed — it is the single most replicated finding in the corpus.

findings.claim: AlphaEvolve's evaluation is an executable function, not an LLM: users provide `evaluate()` returning a dict of scalar scores, and LLM-generated feedback is only an optional supplement for properties code can't measure. The whole pipeline is 'optimized for throughput rather than the speed of any one computation'.
findings.relevance: Our loop inverts this: 50-70% of round cost is LLM judges re-measuring things deterministic code could check via the existing window.__vox / ?test=1 hooks, so the single biggest lever is moving fitness measurement from agents into scripts.

findings.claim: AlphaEvolve uses an explicit evaluation cascade (hypothesis testing): new solutions are first run at small scale to filter faulty programs early, then promoted through test ensembles of increasing difficulty only if they pass earlier stages — expensive evaluation is reserved for survivors.
findings.source: AlphaEvolve paper, Section 2.4 'Evaluation cascade' — same PDF, p. 7
findings.relevance: Maps directly onto restructuring our rating as stages — free scripted boot/regression gate, then delta judge on touched families only, then full 5-seat panel only on disagreement or every Nth round — so evaluation spend is proportional to evidence of change rather than flat per round.

findings.claim: AlphaEvolve found that optimizing multiple metrics simultaneously improves results even on a single target metric, because diverse high-performing examples (different definitions of 'good') in the prompt stimulate more varied candidates; its database combines MAP-Elites with island models to preserve diversity along behavior dimensions.
findings.source: AlphaEvolve paper, Sections 2.4 'Multiple scores' and 2.5 — same PDF, p. 8
findings.relevance: Validates our family-weight ledger and the empirical observation that deltas track family weight opened: per-family machine-logged sub-scores (not just one MPS scalar) should be first-class planner inputs, and unopened families are the MAP-Elites 'empty cells' worth explicit exploration slots.

findings.claim: AlphaEvolve evolves only code regions explicitly marked with EVOLVE-BLOCK-START/END comments inside a frozen skeleton, and LLMs emit targeted SEARCH/REPLACE diff blocks rather than whole-file rewrites; whole-file output is used only when the evolved code is very short.
findings.source: AlphaEvolve paper, Sections 2.1 'API' and 2.3 'Output format' — same PDF, pp. 4-7
findings.relevance: Our 2000-line single HTML file can be annotated into named regions with builders constrained to disjoint-region diffs, which shrinks the regression surface per edit and removes most of the merge hazard that currently forces builders to be serial.

findings.claim: AlphaEvolve pairs Gemini Flash (high-throughput hypothesis volume) with Gemini Pro (occasional higher-quality breakthrough suggestions), and ablations showed removing evolution, large context, meta-prompting, or the larger model each measurably degraded results.
findings.source: AlphaEvolve paper Section 2.3 'Models used' (same PDF, p. 7) + ablations summarized at https://gonzoml.substack.com/p/alphaevolve
findings.relevance: Evidence for cheap-model volume only holds in thousands-of-candidates regimes that our single-lineage loop is not in, and Kevin's global CLAUDE.md explicitly rejects auto-downgrading models — so the transferable part is the ablation lesson (rich context and strong models both matter), not the ensemble routing.

findings.claim: FunSearch's island evolutionary method (k=2 high-scoring parents per prompt, periodic reset of the worst half of islands, discard-on-exception) only worked because it evolved a tiny priority function inside a fixed skeleton with a fast automated evaluator, sampling candidates at massive scale (millions of programs across experiments).
findings.source: Romera-Paredes et al., 'Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models', Nature 2024 — https://www.researchgate.net/publication/376546904 ; island mechanics detailed in https://arxiv.org/pdf/2407.10873
findings.relevance: Anti-transfer: population search pays off when per-candidate evaluation is near-free and candidates are tiny; with our evaluation at 50-70% of round cost and the candidate being a whole game, best-of-N whole-game rounds would multiply the dominant cost — the only transferable piece is scoping evolution to small marked regions.

findings.claim: AIDE frames improvement as tree search with three operators (draft / debug / improve), a hard-coded selection policy (draft a few diverse roots first, bound debug attempts per buggy node, then always improve the best non-buggy node), exactly one atomic change per improve step, and a summarization operator Σ(T) so each node is evaluated statelessly without dragging full history into context.
findings.source: AIDE: AI-Driven Exploration in the Space of Code — https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.13138 (HTML version fetched)
findings.relevance: Supports keeping our one-feature-per-builder 'conservatism' (it is AIDE's atomic-change rule, a feature not a bug), replacing fixed 3-lane rounds with expand-the-best-node logic (lane count set by projected_next_gain), and treating CAMPAIGN.md as the Σ(T) summary that keeps builders stateless.

findings.claim: Reflexion converts scalar reward into verbal 'semantic gradient' reflections stored in a persistent episodic memory buffer that is replayed into later trials; self-reflection added ~8% absolute over episodic memory alone, with no weight updates.
findings.source: Shinn et al., 'Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning', NeurIPS 2023 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366
findings.relevance: Our CAMPAIGN.md already does this for planning, but probe knowledge is not persisted — a durable probe map (feature → test hook → last-verified-round → known quirks) would stop paying the full exploratory-probing cost every round and turn probe findings into compounding memory.

findings.claim: Self-Refine's gains (~20% across tasks) come from feedback being specific and actionable rather than from more refinement rounds, and refined outputs are not always better than originals — fixed iteration counts waste compute and need stopping criteria.
findings.source: Madaan et al., 'Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback' — https://openreview.net/pdf?id=S37hOerQLB
findings.relevance: Judges should emit family-tagged actionable critiques (what to change) rather than scores alone, and the planner should skip building or rating a family when projected_next_gain falls below its token cost — a per-family stopping rule instead of flat rounds.

findings.claim: CodeMonkeys measured that serial iterations and parallel sampling give similar coverage at equal cost, but parallel scaling creates a selection problem that loses about half the oracle gap (57.4% achieved vs 69.8% oracle coverage vs 45.8% random), while serial scaling eliminates selection entirely; selection itself was cheap (5.8% of cost) only because it voted with model-WRITTEN executable tests, and the expensive context scan was amortized across 10 downstream candidates (14.6% of cost).
findings.source: Ehrlich et al., 'CodeMonkeys: Scaling Test-Time Compute for Software Engineering' — https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.14723 (HTML version fetched)
findings.relevance: Two lessons for us: serial builders are defensible (parallel candidates would force our expensive judges to also pay a selection tax), and the amortization trick says build expensive context artifacts (probe map, region map) once and reuse them across builders and rounds.

findings.claim: On SWE-bench-style tasks, running existing regression suites against candidate patches measurably improves resolution by filtering breaking changes, but suites sometimes falsely reject correct patches, so test feedback works best as strong guidance plus a human/agent escape hatch rather than an absolute gate.
findings.source: 'When Old Meets New: Evaluating the Impact of Regression Tests on SWE Issue Resolution' — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.18270
findings.relevance: Directly endorses replacing our expensive adversarial-regression-hunter-every-round with an accumulated scripted regression suite run at near-zero cost each round, while keeping an agent override path for suite false-positives (e.g., a feature legitimately redesigned).

recommendations: Freeze verification into an executable regression suite (highest leverage). Every time the probe verifies a feature, persist that probe sequence as a deterministic window.__vox script in the repo. Run the full scripted suite as a free stage-0 gate every round (boot-gate extension); demote the adversarial regression hunter to every 3rd round and to novel feature-interactions only. This attacks the 50-70% rating cost directly and converts 'verification conversion' from a recurring delta source into compounding capital. Keep an agent escape hatch for suite false-positives per the regression-test study.

Restructure rating as an AlphaEvolve-style evaluation cascade: stage 0 = scripted boot + regression suite (no tokens); stage 1 = delta judge restricted to families actually touched this round, fed the stage-0 results; stage 2 = full 5-seat panel only when stage 1 disagrees with the planner's projected_next_gain by more than a threshold, or on the existing every-3rd-round cadence. Evaluation spend becomes proportional to evidence of change.

Persist a probe map (Reflexion episodic memory + CodeMonkeys amortization): a durable PROBE_MAP file mapping feature -> __vox test hook -> last-verified-round -> known quirks, updated incrementally. Probes then only explore deltas (new features and changed regions) instead of re-discovering the game every round. Same amortization applies to a one-time region/architecture map of the 2000-line file for builders.

Replace fixed 3-lane rounds with AIDE-style expand-the-best-node lane allocation: the planner sets 1-5 builder slots per round from projected_next_gain per family, with explicit bounded debug attempts per feature (strike policy already exists; bind it per-node). Keep one-atomic-change-per-builder — AIDE's results say that discipline is why tree search stays debuggable and sample-efficient, so do not relax it in pursuit of bigger rounds.

Do NOT adopt population / best-of-N whole-candidate search (FunSearch, AlphaEvolve population mechanics, CodeMonkeys parallel axis). Those regimes assume near-free evaluators and tiny evolved units; with LLM judges as the dominant cost, N candidates multiplies exactly the wrong line item and adds a selection gap that cost CodeMonkeys roughly half its oracle headroom. Scoped exception: best-of-2 diffs for one risky subsystem, selected purely by the free scripted suite (never by judges).

Partition the file with AlphaEvolve-style EVOLVE-BLOCK region markers and require SEARCH/REPLACE-style diffs scoped to named regions. Once builders touch provably disjoint regions, 2 builders can safely run in parallel; and since the single-file constraint is on the ARTIFACT, consider developing as modules in the working tree with a deterministic bundle step emitting the canonical HTML at commit time, which removes the merge hazard entirely.
reports.summary: Across AlphaEvolve, FunSearch, AIDE, Reflexion, Self-Refine, CodeMonkeys, and SWE-bench regression-test research, the consistent sample-efficiency story is: (1) the fitness function is executable code, with LLM judgment only for what code cannot measure — our loop spends 50-70% of round cost on LLM judges re-measuring scriptable facts via hooks we already have; (2) evaluation is cascaded so cheap stages prune before expensive stages run; (3) expensive context (exploration, codebase maps) is built once and amortized, whereas our probing is re-done every round; (4) population/best-of-N methods only pay off when evaluation is near-free and the evolved unit is tiny, so they do NOT transfer to our judge-dominated, whole-game loop — CodeMonkeys shows parallel candidates add a selection tax that serial improvement avoids; (5) AIDE validates our one-atomic-change-per-builder discipline while arguing lane count should follow expected value of the best node, not a fixed 3. Net: the loop's biggest wins are turning verified features into a permanent free regression suite, cascading the rating, and persisting the probe map — not adding candidate parallelism."

findings.claim: Two-tier sensor split: 'computational' checks (tests, linters, type checkers, structural rules) run in milliseconds-to-seconds with reliable results and should run on EVERY change, while 'inferential' checks (LLM-as-judge, AI review agents) are slow, expensive, and non-deterministic and belong post-integration only ('keep quality left'). Fowler also flags the key weakness: this 'puts a lot of faith into AI-generated tests, that's not good enough yet' — the behavioral harness remains the unsolved part.
findings.relevance: Our loop pays inferential prices (delta judge + adversarial hunter, 50-70% of round cost) for checks that are mostly computational in nature; a deterministic assertion layer over window.__vox should run at the boot gate before any judge tokens are spent, with the caveat that agent-written tests themselves need auditing.

findings.claim: Capability evals 'graduate' into regression suites: capability evals start at low pass rates and measure what the agent can newly do; once passing, they convert to regression evals expected to hold ~100% and run continuously to catch drift. Code-based graders are 'fast, cheap, objective, reproducible'; LLM graders need only occasional human calibration once robust. Also: don't assert exact step sequences ('too rigid... overly brittle tests') — assert outcomes.
findings.relevance: This is the exact mechanism our loop is missing: every feature the judges credit (our 'verification conversion') should be frozen at crediting time as a code-based regression check, converting the recurring adversarial-hunter cost into a one-time test-authoring cost per feature; tests should assert world-state outcomes via __vox, not input sequences.

findings.claim: In Anthropic's planner/generator/evaluator harness for multi-hour autonomous builds, the evaluator drives the RUNNING app via Playwright MCP 'the way a user would', grades against explicit per-criterion hard thresholds (any one below threshold fails the sprint), and the measured cost ratio is QA ~$3.09-3.24 per round vs build ~$36.89-71.08 per round — evaluation is roughly 5-9% of build cost in a tuned harness. Calibration matters: 'Out of the box, Claude is a poor QA agent' that finds real issues then talks itself out of them; it took several tuning rounds before grading was reasonable. Known gap: it missed layout issues and deeply nested features.
findings.relevance: Our cost structure is inverted relative to a tuned harness (rating 50-70% vs their ~5-9%); the fix is moving most verification into scripted, threshold-gated app-driving rather than fresh judge reasoning each round — while keeping the human checkpoint because even tuned evaluators miss feel/layout issues.

findings.claim: Factorio's regression discipline: (1) every discovered bug must be covered by a failing test BEFORE it is fixed (a QA lead's primary role was enforcing this); (2) GUI tests synthesize low-level input events so all event-processing layers are exercised even when run without graphics; (3) tests run in dependency order so a failure points at the root cause rather than cascading; (4) net effect: 'working with tests feels slower but it is actually faster.' Companion posts describe CRC checks of game state after each test to catch determinism regressions automatically.
findings.source: https://www.factorio.com/blog/post/fff-366 (and https://www.factorio.com/blog/post/fff-62 for CRC/daily automated test releases)
findings.relevance: Directly transferable to a deterministic voxel sim: every regression the hunter or human checkpoint finds becomes a permanent replay test before builders fix it, and a post-script world-state hash (CRC-style) over __vox gives a near-zero-token determinism ratchet for physics/redstone/mob behavior.

findings.claim: Long-horizon iterative coding agents systematically break previously passing features as iterations accumulate (compounding subtle bugs, context limits losing system-wide implications); the mitigations that measurably help are maintained test suites giving immediate regression feedback, checkpointed states for rollback, and validation loops that catch regressions early — but the validation overhead itself is a real cost the paper highlights.
findings.relevance: Confirms our regression exposure is structural, not incidental: 3 serial builders per round editing one 2000-line file with no automated suite is the worst case the paper describes, and the immediate-feedback mitigation (suite runs after EACH builder, not once per round) is what bounds compounding.

findings.claim: Persistent contextual rubrics are a cheap reusable verifier class for SWE agent outputs: rubrics generated once per change-context and reused are reported at ~$0.293/instance vs ~$0.515 for test generation and ~$0.736 for patch similarity, while catching issues similarity-based approaches miss — cheap verification without proportional compute growth.
findings.source: Agentic Rubrics as Contextual Verifiers for SWE Agents, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.04171 (per-instance dollar figures as reported in abstract/search index; fetch confirmed the direction, not the exact figures)
findings.relevance: Our judges re-derive evaluation context every round; persisting a per-family rubric file (redstone, mobs, etc.) in CAMPAIGN.md's orbit and grading against it would cut the delta judge's per-round reasoning cost roughly in half by this paper's economics.

findings.claim: Golden-master / record-and-replay practice for games: record an input stream once against a deterministic engine, replay it headlessly on every build, and compare a captured snapshot of resulting state; bugs become 100% reproducible and 'every new build could play itself from the first level through the final boss.' Used in shipped games (Retro City Rampage GDC talk) and standard characterization-testing literature.
findings.relevance: Our probe agent re-explores the world every round at intelligent-agent prices; recording its session once into a replayable __vox input script makes prior coverage replay mechanically in seconds, reserving live-probe tokens for only the family opened this round.

findings.claim: Eval placement doctrine: 'if evals do not run on every change, they do not exist — evaluation belongs in the pipeline next to lint, type-check, and build,' and agent-authored PR studies find CI failures and regressions are the top rejection reasons for agent code.
findings.relevance: Argues the regression suite must gate at the per-builder commit boundary inside the round (alongside our existing boot gate), not run as a separate rating-phase activity.

recommendations: Build a deterministic replay harness as a SIBLING file in the working tree (the single-file constraint binds the artifact, not the repo): a headless-Chromium runner that boots the canonical HTML with ?test=1, executes scripted input sequences against window.__vox, asserts world-state outcomes (not input steps), and computes a Factorio-style post-script state hash. Run it after EACH serial builder as part of the boot gate — near-zero token cost, bounds the compounding-regression failure mode SlopCodeBench documents.

Adopt capability-to-regression graduation as a hard loop rule: a feature's MPS credit at verification-conversion time is provisional until the crediting round also emits a deterministic replay test for it. This converts the adversarial regression hunter's recurring per-round cost into a one-time test-authoring cost per feature, exactly the 'graduate to regression suite' mechanism Anthropic describes.

Enforce the Factorio bug-first rule: any regression found by the hunter, judges, or the human checkpoint must be encoded as a failing replay test before builders may fix it. Then re-charter the hunter: instead of re-hunting old surface every round, it audits the test suite for blind spots and probes only newly opened family surface — this also answers Fowler's warning about over-trusting agent-written tests (the hunter becomes the test-auditor).

Record-and-replay the probe: have the live probe agent emit its session as a replayable input script each round; subsequent rounds replay accumulated scripts mechanically in seconds and spend intelligent-probe tokens only on the new family built that round. This kills the 'exploratory probing re-done every round' inefficiency via the standard golden-master pattern from shipped games.

Re-budget rating against Anthropic's measured ratio (QA ~5-9% of build cost in a tuned harness vs our 50-70%): once the deterministic suite exists, feed the delta judge the suite's pass/fail ledger plus evidence of the new feature only, with hard per-criterion thresholds; keep the full 5-seat panel on every-3rd-round cadence but give it the ledger instead of re-verification work. Target rating at or under ~20% of round cost. Do NOT try to eliminate LLM judging entirely — MPS fidelity ('feels like Minecraft', visuals, juice) is exactly the part code-based graders cannot score, and tuned evaluators still miss layout/feel issues, so the human checkpoint stays.

Persist per-family rubrics (redstone, mobs, etc.) as files the planner maintains and judges grade against, rather than re-deriving criteria each round — the Agentic Rubrics economics suggest roughly 40%+ cheaper verification per instance, and it makes delta-judge scores comparable across rounds. Skeptical note: two popular patterns that do NOT transfer — (a) mutation testing / coverage tooling assumes a test-framework codebase, overkill for one 2000-line HTML file with an in-page hook API; (b) fully replacing judges with tests fails here because the fitness function (MPS vs real Minecraft) is partly aesthetic judgment, so the correct split is: tests own regression, judges own novelty and fidelity.
reports.summary: Verification economics angle: the literature converges on a two-tier doctrine our loop currently violates. Cheap deterministic checks (replay tests, state hashes, outcome assertions) should run on every change at near-zero token cost, while expensive LLM judgment should be reserved for what code cannot grade — novelty and fidelity. Anthropic's tuned planner/generator/evaluator harness measures QA at ~5-9% of build cost; ours is 50-70%, an inverted ratio caused by paying intelligent-agent prices for mechanical regression checking and re-done exploration. The three load-bearing mechanisms to import: (1) capability-to-regression graduation — every judge-credited feature is frozen as a deterministic replay test at crediting time, turning the regression hunter's recurring cost into a one-time cost; (2) Factorio's bug-first rule plus post-script world-state hashing, which a deterministic voxel sim with existing __vox/?test=1 hooks is unusually well positioned to adopt (the suite lives in a sibling harness file since the single-file constraint binds the artifact, not the working tree); (3) golden-master record-and-replay of the probe agent's sessions so prior coverage replays mechanically and live-probe tokens go only to newly opened families. SlopCodeBench confirms regressions compound structurally in long-horizon agent loops and that immediate per-edit suite feedback (after each serial builder, not once per round) is the bounding mitigation. What does not transfer: eliminating judges entirely (MPS is partly aesthetic; tests own regression, judges own novelty/fidelity), and heavyweight coverage/mutation tooling. Guard the ratchet per Fowler's warning about agent-written tests by re-chartering the adversarial hunter as a test-suite auditor, and keep the human checkpoint for feel/layout blind spots that even tuned evaluators miss.

findings.claim: Cognition's core mechanism against parallel code-writing agents: 'actions carry implicit decisions, and conflicting decisions carry bad results.' Their canonical failure is two parallel subagents building a Flappy Bird clone where one chose a Mario-style background and the other an incompatible bird sprite, each reasonable in isolation. Recommended default: single-threaded linear agent with a context-compression model for long tasks; parallelism only becomes viable when agents can negotiate conflicts, which current systems cannot do reliably.
findings.relevance: Directly validates our serial-builder decision for interdependent gameplay systems in one file; naively parallelizing the 3 builders would produce physics/style/API inconsistencies that the adversarial regression hunter would then expensively rediscover.

findings.claim: Anthropic's measured multi-agent economics: parallel subagent systems consume ~15x the tokens of chat (~4x for a single agent), token usage alone explains 80% of performance variance, and they explicitly note 'most coding tasks involve fewer truly parallelizable tasks than research' — parallelism pays on breadth-first, read-only exploration. They also embed explicit effort-scaling rules (1 agent/3-10 calls for simple tasks, 10+ subagents only for genuinely decomposable work) to prevent overinvestment.
findings.relevance: Parallelism buys breadth and wall-clock, not token efficiency — and tokens are our binding resource at 1.7-2.2M/MPS point; it argues for parallelizing the read-only probe/verification stages and for replacing our fixed 3-lane rounds with planner-set effort scaling.

findings.claim: CodeMonkeys (Stanford) best-of-N mechanism with numbers: 10 parallel edit+test trajectories per issue, selection via test-based voting (filter to top-3) then a model-based selector that can write new discriminating tests. Selection recovered ~half the gap between random pick (45.8%) and oracle (69.8%), landing at 57.4% — and the selection stage cost only 5.8% of the $2,300 total because model-generated executable tests did the filtering, not an expensive judge.
findings.source: https://arxiv.org/html/2501.14723v1 (CodeMonkeys: Scaling Test-Time Compute for Software Engineering)
findings.relevance: Best-of-N only pays when the selector is cheap and automated; in our loop the natural selector (the judge panel) is already 50-70% of round cost, so best-of-N is a net loss unless candidates are selected by automated ?test=1 probe metrics and boot-gates instead of judges.

findings.claim: CodeMonkeys iso-cost frontier: after the first few iterations/trajectories, configurations with similar total inference cost achieve similar coverage regardless of how compute is split between serial depth and parallel width — but the equivalence breaks at final score, because many candidates make selection harder while a single deep trajectory 'eliminates the selection problem entirely.'
findings.source: https://arxiv.org/html/2501.14723v1 (Section on serial vs parallel scaling)
findings.relevance: Our 3 serial deep builders are not obviously leaving coverage on the table versus parallel candidates at equal token spend; the case for going parallel rests entirely on having cheap automated selection, not on parallelism itself.

findings.claim: AlphaEvolve's loop is parallel-candidate generation gated by automated evaluation cascades: candidate programs face increasingly challenging tests so weak candidates are discarded early and cheaply, only survivors get expensive evaluation; Gemini Flash maximizes breadth of mutations while Pro provides depth; a programs database feeds the best performers back into prompts. The entire architecture is predicated on evaluators being automated and objective.
findings.relevance: The cascade pattern maps directly onto our rating redundancy: a free deterministic gate (boot + window.__vox assertions + FPS + feature checklist) should run before any LLM judging, so the expensive panel only ever scores candidates that already pass the cheap tier — attacking the 50-70% rating share harder than any builder parallelism would.

findings.claim: Practitioner lessons from running parallel coding agents (battyterm): (1) test-gating before merge — agents cannot mark work done until the suite exits 0 — cut agent-induced breakage ~80%; (2) worktree isolation is non-negotiable because same-directory agents silently overwrite each other; (3) 'central registry' files that every feature touches guarantee merge conflicts even with only 3 agents; (4) parallel agents convert the build bottleneck into a review-throughput bottleneck (10 agents = 10 diffs/hour to review).
findings.relevance: Our single 2000-line HTML file is the degenerate 'central registry' case — 100% of edits touch it, so worktree fan-out without sharding just defers collisions to merge time; and any parallelization without an automated regression suite as the merge gate shifts cost into the expensive regression-hunter agent.

findings.claim: Dolthub's parallel-agent practice: isolated containers/branches per agent, a practical ceiling of 3-4 concurrent agents on a 32GB machine before resource pressure crashes sessions, work decomposed into measured sequential steps (reproduce → failing test → fix → validate) rather than end-to-end autonomy, and the summary judgment that 'testing infrastructure is everything' — deterministic reference comparisons are what let agents validate without hallucinating progress.
findings.relevance: Confirms a low hardware-bounded parallelism ceiling on a single machine (matching the M1 constraint) and that our deterministic ?test=1 sim hooks are the strategic asset to grow into a regression suite — the enabler that every successful parallel setup cites.

findings.claim: Single-file artifacts do not require single-file working trees: vite-plugin-singlefile (and equivalents) deterministically inline all JS/CSS modules into one dist/index.html via cssCodeSplit, assetsInlineLimit, and inlineDynamicImports, purpose-built for double-click-to-run offline HTML apps.
findings.relevance: This is the unlock for module-sharding: split source into per-family modules (worldgen, redstone, mobs, render, UI) in the working tree, bundle to the canonical single HTML at commit time, and the merge hazard drops from textual (every edit collides) to semantic only — making worktree fan-out across disjoint families actually safe.

findings.claim: The 'Chairman' best-of-N pattern (Blackbox AI, documented in hermes-agent issue #479): dispatch the same task to N agents in parallel, have an independent judge (not one of the competitors) select the winner; diversity should come from model/approach variation. The worktree variant: two agents rewrite the same file with different approaches and review picks the winner — selection replaces merging entirely.
findings.relevance: For a one-file codebase, competition-not-collaboration sidesteps merge cost completely: applicable to our single highest-risk lane per round (new family openers like redstone), where two full-file candidates get selected by automated probe metrics — but it doubles build tokens, so it only pays on lanes with high outcome variance.

findings.claim: Inter-agent misalignment accounts for 32.3% of observed multi-agent system failures in the MAST taxonomy cited in the Cognition-vs-Anthropic debate — roughly one in three failures is a class that cannot occur in a single-agent architecture.
findings.relevance: Quantifies the hidden tax of parallelizing our builders: a third of new failure modes would be coordination-induced, and in our loop every induced regression costs an expensive hunter-agent pass plus possible MPS clawback in the next rating.

recommendations: Shard the working tree, keep the artifact single-file. Split the 2000-line HTML into per-family source modules (worldgen, physics, redstone, mobs, render, UI) with a deterministic bundle step (vite-plugin-singlefile, or a ~50-line concat script for vanilla JS) emitting the canonical HTML; boot-gate and probe always run against the built artifact. This converts the merge hazard from textual to semantic and is the precondition for any builder parallelism. Until this lands, keep builders serial — the evidence says parallel edits to a 'central registry' file guarantee conflict cost that exceeds the wall-clock savings.

Build the automated regression suite BEFORE parallelizing anything, and treat it as the merge gate. Practitioner data shows test-gating cuts agent-induced breakage ~80%, and every successful parallel/best-of-N system (CodeMonkeys, AlphaEvolve, Dolthub) is built on cheap automated evaluation. Cheapest path: persist the probe agent's ?test=1 transcripts as replayable window.__vox assertion scripts, so each round's exploration compounds into the suite instead of being re-done — this simultaneously kills the repeated-exploration inefficiency and shrinks the adversarial regression hunter's job.

Insert an evaluation cascade in front of the judge panel (AlphaEvolve pattern): tier 0 = boot-gate + automated assertions + FPS budget (free), tier 1 = delta judge on changed families only, tier 2 = full 5-seat panel every Nth round. Nothing reaches a paid judge without passing the free tier. Rating is 50-70% of round cost; this is where tokens-per-MPS-point improves fastest, more than any builder-side parallelism.

Use best-of-2 competition only on the single highest-variance lane per round — new family openers, where deltas actually come from — with selection by automated probe metrics plus the cheap delta judge, never the full panel. CodeMonkeys shows selection recovers ~half the oracle gap at ~6% cost ONLY when selection is test-based; with judge-based selection the math inverts in our loop. Do not best-of-N polish/consolidation lanes: deltas track family weight opened, so extra candidates there buy nothing.

Parallelize the read-only stages, not the writers: run probe play-verification, regression hunting, and judge evidence-gathering concurrently (breadth-first, zero merge cost — Anthropic's documented sweet spot), while builders stay serial or, post-sharding, fan out only across provably disjoint family modules with the regression suite gating each merge into an integration branch before the canonical commit.

Replace fixed 3-lane rounds with planner-set effort scaling (Anthropic's explicit effort-rules pattern): the planner already logs projected_next_gain, so let it choose lane count and round shape — verification-conversion rounds get 1 builder + heavy probing; family-opening rounds get 2-3 lanes (or one best-of-2 lane); and consider batching two build rounds per full panel rating with only the free cascade between, since CodeMonkeys' iso-cost frontier implies how compute is distributed matters less than total spend until selection/rating costs dominate — which in our loop they already do.
reports.summary: For our loop, the evidence says builder parallelism is the wrong first move: a single 2000-line file is the degenerate 'central registry' case where worktree fan-out merely defers guaranteed conflicts, Cognition's implicit-decision conflicts and the 32% inter-agent failure share land directly on interdependent gameplay code, and Anthropic's 15x token multiplier means parallelism spends our binding resource (tokens) to buy wall-clock we don't need. The transferable wins are: (1) shard the source tree and bundle deterministically to the single-file artifact, which makes future fan-out safe; (2) convert probe transcripts into an automated regression suite and put a free evaluation cascade in front of the judge panel, attacking the 50-70% rating cost that dominates tokens-per-MPS; (3) reserve best-of-N competition (selection-replaces-merge) for the one high-variance family-opening lane per round with automated selection, since CodeMonkeys shows selection only pays when it costs ~6%, not when the selector is your most expensive component; and (4) parallelize the read-only probe/verification stages where merge cost is zero, while moving from fixed 3-lane rounds to planner-set effort scaling keyed to projected gain.

ranked_changes.change: Graduate every credited feature into a deterministic __vox regression suite that gates each builder commit (capability-to-regression graduation + bug-first rule + test ratchet)
ranked_changes.mechanism: Add a sibling harness in the working tree (tests/replay_runner + tests/features/*) that boots the canonical HTML headlessly with ?test=1, executes scripted window.__vox sequences, asserts world-state OUTCOMES (never input-step sequences), and computes a Factorio-style post-script world-state hash. Loop rules: (a) one-time initializer pass backfills a suite entry with a passes boolean for every feature CAMPAIGN.md currently marks verified; (b) thereafter, MPS credit at verification-conversion is provisional until the crediting round also emits the feature's replay test; (c) any regression found by hunter, judges, or the human checkpoint becomes a failing replay test BEFORE builders may fix it; (d) the suite runs after EACH serial builder as an extension of the existing boot-gate, full suite at round boot; (e) builder prompts get a ratchet line — never weaken or delete a __vox check to make a feature pass — with an escape hatch for legitimate redesigns requiring planner sign-off logged in CAMPAIGN.md.
ranked_changes.expected_effect: Converts the two largest recurring rating costs — verification-conversion re-checks (a measured big delta component) and regression hunting over old surface — into near-zero-token script runs, and bounds the compounding-regression failure mode of 3 serial editors on one 2000-line file, protecting the banked 45.4 MPS from clawback. Crediting moves to the same round a feature is built, pulling verification-conversion deltas forward. Estimated 10-20% of the ~1.1-1.5M round saved directly, and it is the prerequisite that makes changes 2, 3, and 8 possible. This is the strongest cross-report consensus item (all four angles independently rank it first).
ranked_changes.cost_to_adopt: One-time backfill initializer ~150-400K tokens (about a third of one round); ~5-15K tokens per feature at crediting time thereafter; small runner script, no new heavy deps (headless Chromium is already implied by the existing boot-gate/probe). Risk: agent-authored tests can be wrong or vacuous — mitigated by outcome-not-step assertions and by re-chartering the hunter as suite auditor (change 2).
ranked_changes.evidence: Practitioner: Anthropic long-running-harness feature-list JSON with passes booleans; Ralph backpressure ('if it can't be autovalidated, don't loop it'); test ratchet (Osmani). Verification-economics: Anthropic capability-to-regression graduation; Factorio bug-first rule + CRC state hashing; SlopCodeBench per-edit suite feedback as the bounding mitigation; eval-placement doctrine (gate at the commit boundary). Research-systems: AlphaEvolve executable evaluate(); SWE-bench regression study (suites help, keep an escape hatch). Parallelization: battyterm test-gating cut agent breakage ~80%; Dolthub 'testing infrastructure is everything'.

ranked_changes.change: Restructure rating as a three-stage evaluation cascade so judge tokens are proportional to evidence of change; re-charter the regression hunter as a suite auditor; add a frozen-build judge-drift oracle
ranked_changes.mechanism: Stage 0 (free): boot-gate + full regression suite + FPS budget + world-state hash, results written to RATING_LEDGER.json. Stage 1: the existing delta judge, scoped to families actually touched this round, fed the stage-0 ledger plus new-feature evidence instead of doing its own re-verification; it must emit family-tagged actionable critiques, not scores alone. Stage 2: the full 5-seat panel fires only on the existing every-3rd-round cadence OR when stage-1's score disagrees with CAMPAIGN.md's projected_next_gain beyond a threshold — and it receives the ledger rather than re-verifying. The adversarial regression hunter stops re-hunting old surface every round: it audits the suite for blind spots and probes only newly opened family surface, full-breadth every 3rd round (this also answers the over-trusting-agent-written-tests risk from change 1). Periodically re-score a frozen known-good build as a drift oracle for the incremental judges (Carlini's GCC-oracle analog).
ranked_changes.expected_effect: The direct attack on the measured 50-70% rating share — the single largest line item. Anthropic's tuned harness measures QA at ~5-9% of build cost; ours is inverted. Realistic target: rating share to 25-35% near-term, toward ~20% once changes 3 and 5 land — roughly 300-600K tokens saved per round at current shape, improving tokens/MPS from 1.7-2.2M toward ~1.2-1.5M holding deltas constant.
ranked_changes.cost_to_adopt: Mostly prompt/protocol rewiring of agents that already exist; disagreement threshold can be calibrated against 11 rounds of logged projected-vs-actual data already in CAMPAIGN.md. Risk of missed regressions on hunter-demoted rounds is bounded by the suite (change 1 is a hard prerequisite). Oracle re-scores cost one extra light judging run every few rounds.
ranked_changes.evidence: Research-systems: AlphaEvolve evaluation cascade (expensive evaluation reserved for survivors); Self-Refine (actionable feedback + stopping criteria, fixed iteration counts waste compute). Verification-economics: Fowler computational/inferential two-tier split; Anthropic harness QA-to-build ratio ~5-9% vs our 50-70%. Practitioner: Carlini sampled fast tier + known-good oracle; Anthropic effort-scaling finding that the fix is rules and deterministic checks, not more agents. Parallelization: cascade-in-front-of-paid-judges as the pattern every successful system shares.

ranked_changes.change: Record-and-replay the probe: persist exploration as replayable __vox scripts plus PROBE_MAP.json so live probing only covers deltas
ranked_changes.mechanism: Each round the live probe agent emits its session as a replayable __vox input script (same format as suite tests, so probe coverage compounds INTO the suite) and updates PROBE_MAP.json (feature -> hook calls -> expected observations -> last-verified-round -> known quirks). Next round's probe phase: mechanically replay accumulated scripts in seconds plus a ~10% random sample (deterministic within a round, random across rounds), and spend live intelligent-probe tokens only on this round's new or changed families. Build a one-time REGION_MAP.md of the 2000-line file, amortized across builders and rounds (CodeMonkeys-style context amortization).
ranked_changes.expected_effect: Eliminates the 'exploratory probing re-done every round' tax, which is pure waste under Carlini's 14:1 input:output ratio (reading/probing is where tokens go). Honest estimate: 10-20% of round cost once script coverage accumulates — small in the first round or two, compounding thereafter. Turns probe findings from a recurring expense into capital.
ranked_changes.cost_to_adopt: Marginal per-round cost for the probe to emit scripts; PROBE_MAP upkeep folds into the existing probe phase. Risk of stale maps is mitigated by the random replay sample and last-verified-round fields. Low risk overall.
ranked_changes.evidence: Research-systems: Reflexion persistent episodic memory (probe knowledge is the one memory our loop does not persist); CodeMonkeys amortizing expensive context across downstream consumers (14.6% of cost, reused 10x). Verification-economics: golden-master record-and-replay from shipped games (Retro City Rampage GDC, characterization testing). Practitioner: Carlini 14:1 ratio + pre-computed summaries; HN re-exploration/context-pollution tax.

ranked_changes.change: Replace fixed 3-lane rounds with planner-set effort scaling keyed to projected_next_gain, plus periodic CAMPAIGN.md regeneration from the ledger
ranked_changes.mechanism: The planner prompt gets explicit allocation rules: lane count (1-4) and rating tier are chosen from CAMPAIGN.md's projected_next_gain per family. Verification-conversion rounds run 1 builder + heavy replay/probe; family-opener rounds run 2-3 lanes; a family is skipped when its projected gain falls below its token cost (per-family stopping rule). Bind bounded debug attempts per feature to the existing strike policy. Calibrate thresholds from the 11 rounds of logged projections vs actual deltas we already possess. On every full-panel round, regenerate CAMPAIGN.md's narrative from the family-weight ledger + debts instead of letting it accrete (keep the ledger, rewrite the prose) — Huntley's 'throw the plan out often' applied with a safety net.
ranked_changes.expected_effect: Our measured data says deltas track family weight opened, not effort — so flat 3-lane allocation provably wastes builder tokens on low-gain rounds (est. 200-400K/round recovered on conversion/polish rounds) and underinvests in openers, where the +1.0/+1.2 rounds came from. Secondary: regeneration fights stale-plan drift across a long marathon and keeps planner input lean.
ranked_changes.cost_to_adopt: Prompt changes only. Main risk is planner misallocation, which is exactly what the Anthropic finding says happens WITHOUT explicit written effort rules — the rules are the mitigation, and bounded per-node attempts cap the downside.
ranked_changes.evidence: Practitioner: Anthropic multi-agent system — agents misallocate effort without explicit scaling rules; token spend explains ~80% of performance variance; Huntley plan-regeneration. Research-systems: AIDE expand-the-best-node selection with bounded debug attempts; Self-Refine stopping criteria. Strongest support is our own measured delta-vs-family-weight relationship — this change just makes the loop act on its own data.

ranked_changes.change: Persist per-family rubrics that judges grade against instead of re-deriving evaluation criteria every round
ranked_changes.mechanism: rubrics/<family>.md (redstone, mobs, worldgen, render, UI, ...) created by the planner when a family opens, reviewed/updated only on full-panel rounds. The delta judge and the panel grade against rubric + RATING_LEDGER.json rather than reconstructing what 'good redstone' means each time. Rubric versions are logged so scores stay comparable across rounds — which also stabilizes the disagreement threshold that triggers stage 2 in change 2.
ranked_changes.expected_effect: Agentic-rubrics economics report ~40% cheaper verification per instance for persisted contextual rubrics vs regenerated evaluation (direction confirmed by the report's fetch, exact dollar figures soft — discount accordingly). Applies to the judge tokens that REMAIN after change 2, so absolute savings are moderate; the underrated benefit is cross-round score comparability, which protects the incremental-judging scheme from drift.
ranked_changes.cost_to_adopt: Trivial: a few K tokens per family at open time, panel-round review for lock-in risk. Near-zero downside.
ranked_changes.evidence: Verification-economics: Agentic Rubrics as Contextual Verifiers (~$0.293/instance vs $0.515 test-gen and $0.736 patch-similarity, flagged as search-index-level figures). Research-systems: Self-Refine — gains come from specific, actionable feedback, not more refinement rounds; multiple-scores finding from AlphaEvolve supports per-family sub-scores as first-class planner inputs.

ranked_changes.change: Deterministic per-builder acceptance gates, log-to-file-then-tail output discipline, and per-phase budget circuit breakers
ranked_changes.mechanism: (a) Probe/builder prompts: redirect sim and console output to files, read back tail/grep summaries only; the harness prints one greppable ERROR line per failure, never thousands of bytes. (b) Before any judge spends a token, each builder lane must pass a deterministic acceptance check: artifact boots clean AND the lane's own feature __vox assertion is green — the script IS the completion condition, deliberately a script and not a 'fast cheap model' checker (mechanical checks get deterministic substitutes, not downgraded models, per both the evidence and the owner's standing no-auto-downgrade rule). (c) Per-phase token budget caps with a circuit breaker that ends the round at a checkpoint commit instead of bleeding into the next phase.
ranked_changes.expected_effect: Honest 5-15% input-token reduction in probe/builder phases (the practitioner ~10x context claim is for chattier workloads than ours — discounted). The acceptance gate prevents the worst per-round waste: judges scoring a lane whose feature never actually worked. Circuit breakers cap tail-risk runaway rounds, which matters since tokens/MPS is already rising.
ranked_changes.cost_to_adopt: Prompt lines plus a tiny gate script; near-zero effort and risk. Adoptable this round, before anything else lands.
ranked_changes.evidence: Practitioner: overnight-run playbook (output-to-file + tail, per-phase budget caps, 'all 5 phases, zero failures'); Carlini terse greppable harness output and 'Claude can't tell time'; Osmani — cost control is genuinely unsolved beyond budgets and circuit breakers; HN iteration/time caps consensus.

ranked_changes.change: Shard the working tree into per-family source modules with a deterministic ~50-line bundle step; the artifact stays single-file
ranked_changes.mechanism: Split the 2000-line HTML into src/ modules (worldgen, physics, render, mobs, redstone, UI) plus a zero-dependency concat/inline script that deterministically emits the canonical single HTML. Boot-gate, suite, and probe always run against the BUILT artifact; the gate additionally asserts bundle byte-determinism. Builders read REGION_MAP.md plus their own module instead of the whole file. Sequence it on a quiet verification-conversion round AFTER change 1 lands, so the full suite gates the refactor itself. (Skip vite-plugin-singlefile — same idea, but a heavy dep we don't need for vanilla JS.)
ranked_changes.expected_effect: Modest immediate saving: each builder stops re-reading ~20K tokens of unrelated file per pass (est. 5-10% of build phase). The real value is converting the merge hazard from textual (every edit collides in the degenerate central-registry file) to semantic-only — the precondition for ever running 2 builders on disjoint families, and for AlphaEvolve-style region-scoped diffs that shrink per-edit regression surface. Claimed here as option value, not savings.
ranked_changes.cost_to_adopt: One focused refactor, roughly half a round of tokens, with real regression risk — the highest-risk item on this list, which is why it ranks below every pure-savings change and is gated behind the suite. The bundle step is a new failure surface, mitigated by gating on the built artifact + determinism check.
ranked_changes.evidence: Parallelization: single-file artifact does not require a single-file working tree (vite-plugin-singlefile existence proof); battyterm central-registry finding; Cognition implicit-decision conflicts as the thing disjoint modules prevent. Research-systems: AlphaEvolve EVOLVE-BLOCK regions + SEARCH/REPLACE scoped diffs inside a frozen skeleton.

ranked_changes.change: Conditional, gated last: best-of-2 candidates on the single family-opener lane, selected only by the free suite + scripted probe metrics, never by judges
ranked_changes.mechanism: Only on rounds opening a new family (the measured delta source), run two builder candidates for that one lane; stage-0 suite + scripted ?test=1 probe metrics (at most the cheap delta judge as tiebreaker) pick the winner; the loser is discarded without panel review. Hard gate: first instrument opener failure/stall rate in CAMPAIGN.md for ~3 rounds; adopt only if openers fail or stall at least roughly 1-in-4 attempts. Never apply to polish or consolidation lanes — measured deltas say extra candidates there buy nothing.
ranked_changes.expected_effect: Insurance against the most expensive single failure our economics allow: a failed opener wastes a ~1.1-1.5M-token round for ~0 MPS, while the second candidate costs ~200-400K. Net-positive ONLY under the measured failure condition; if instrumentation shows openers usually land, skip this permanently. Explicitly not a general best-of-N adoption.
ranked_changes.cost_to_adopt: +1 builder's tokens on opener rounds; selection must stay deterministic or the math inverts (CodeMonkeys: selection pays only when it costs ~6%, and our judges are the most expensive component). Depends on changes 1 and 2 having landed.
ranked_changes.evidence: Parallelization + research-systems: CodeMonkeys selection economics (test-based selection 5.8% of cost, recovered ~half the oracle gap; judge-based selection inverts in our loop); AlphaEvolve cascade-priced survivors; Chairman pattern scoped to selection-replaces-merge. Adjudicated against our measured family-weight-drives-deltas data, which is what restricts it to opener lanes.

rejected.idea: Parallel builder fan-out now (worktrees + lock files + model-mediated merges, Anthropic C-compiler style)
rejected.why: It spends our binding resource to buy one we have plenty of: tokens, not wall-clock, bind this loop (multi-agent setups burn ~15x tokens; token spend explains ~80% of performance variance), merge-agent tokens are pure overhead, and 32.3% of multi-agent failures are inter-agent misalignment — a failure class that cannot occur serially and that our expensive hunter would then pay to rediscover. The single 2000-line file is the degenerate 'central registry' where 100% of edits collide, so worktree fan-out just defers conflicts to merge time. The 16-agent proof-of-feasibility is real but optimized for throughput on a $20K budget; our objective is MPS per token. Revisit only after sharding (change 7) and only if wall-time ever becomes binding.

rejected.idea: Best-of-N / population / island evolutionary search at whole-game granularity (FunSearch, AlphaEvolve population mechanics, Chairman-everywhere)
rejected.why: Those regimes are predicated on near-free evaluators and tiny evolved units sampled at massive scale. Our evaluator is LLM judges at 50-70% of round cost and the candidate is an entire game — N candidates multiplies exactly the dominant line item, and CodeMonkeys shows parallel candidates additionally lose about half the oracle headroom to the selection problem. Only the narrowly scoped, suite-selected best-of-2 on opener lanes survives adjudication, and even that is gated on instrumented failure-rate data (ranked change 8).

rejected.idea: Replace LLM judges entirely with deterministic tests
rejected.why: MPS is scored against real Minecraft and is partly aesthetic — feel, juice, visual fidelity — which code-based graders cannot measure, and even Anthropic's tuned Playwright evaluator still missed layout and deeply nested issues. The correct split the evidence supports: tests own regression, judges own novelty and fidelity, and the scarce human checkpoint stays for real-input-path blind spots that headless agents structurally cannot observe. The goal is rating at ~20-30% of round cost, not zero.

rejected.idea: Downgrade to cheap/fast models for judging, probing, or completion-checking (Gemini-Flash-style volume routing, fast-model goal checkers)
rejected.why: Cheap-model volume pays only in thousands-of-candidates regimes (AlphaEvolve generates and discards en masse; its own ablations show removing the strong model degraded results). We run a single lineage where each judgment steers a 1M+-token round — a wrong cheap verdict costs far more than the model savings. Where a check is mechanical, the right substitution is a deterministic script at zero cost (ranked change 6), not a cheaper model. This also matches the owner's standing rule rejecting auto-downgrading models for mechanical work.

rejected.idea: Heavyweight new dependencies: Playwright/Puppeteer MCP harness, vite-plugin-singlefile, coverage/mutation testing tooling
rejected.why: Violates the no-new-heavy-deps constraint and adds nothing we lack: window.__vox + ?test=1 + deterministic sim hooks already provide app-driving and observability, so a minimal headless runner suffices; a ~50-line concat script replaces the bundler for vanilla JS; mutation/coverage tooling presumes a test-framework codebase and is overkill for one HTML artifact with an in-page hook API — the replay suite delivers the same regression guarantee for free.

rejected.idea: Relax one-feature-per-builder into bigger multi-feature lanes ('fix' the conservatism)
rejected.why: This is the single most replicated finding across all four reports and it points the other way: Ralph's 'one item per loop' (repeated verbatim), AIDE's one-atomic-change operator (why tree search stays debuggable and sample-efficient), and Anthropic's harness practice all converge on atomic units. SlopCodeBench shows our regression exposure is structural, and bigger edits widen exactly the surface our most expensive phase pays to audit. Scale ambition via lane count and family selection (ranked change 4), never via lane size.

rejected.idea: Improve rating fidelity by adding more judge seats or more adversarial hunter agents
rejected.why: That is the measured cost direction to avoid: rating is already 50-70% of round cost and the multi-agent evidence says performance scales with token spend, which is precisely what we cannot afford at 1.7-2.2M tokens/MPS and rising. The loop already moved correctly from 5 seats to delta+hunter; fidelity gains should come from better deterministic evidence flowing into FEWER judge tokens (suite ledger, rubrics, frozen-build drift oracle), not more inferential seats.

rejected.idea: Batch two or more build rounds per rating pass / drop per-round delta judging
rejected.why: Per-round delta feedback is what calibrates the planner's projected_next_gain — the very signal ranked change 4 depends on — and catches scoring drift before it compounds across builders. Once the cascade lands, the per-round delta judge is already cheap and ledger-fed, so batching saves little while blinding the loop between checkpoints; the suite handles regressions, not scoring. The CodeMonkeys iso-cost argument cuts the other way here: when distribution of spend matters less than total spend, keep the cheap feedback every round.

synthesis.summary: All four research angles converge on the same diagnosis: the loop's shape is already best-practice (planner + persistent ledger, serial one-feature builders, judges separated from builders, scarce human checkpoint), and the inefficiency is paying inferential prices for computational checks — rating at 50-70% of round cost versus ~5-9% in tuned harnesses. The ranked changes therefore flip verification economics first: (1) graduate every credited feature into a deterministic __vox replay suite that gates each builder commit, (2) cascade rating so judge tokens are proportional to evidence of change with the hunter re-chartered as suite auditor, (3) record-and-replay the probe so exploration compounds instead of repeating. Then tune allocation against our own measured data — deltas track family weight opened, not effort — via planner-set lane counts keyed to projected_next_gain, persisted per-family rubrics, and cheap output/budget discipline. Structural moves come last and gated: source-tree sharding (artifact stays single-file via a 50-line bundle script) for option value, and suite-selected best-of-2 only on family-opener lanes only if instrumentation shows openers actually fail. Rejected wholesale: builder parallelism, broad best-of-N/population search, judge elimination, cheap-model downgrades, heavy deps, multi-feature lanes, more judge seats, and rating-cadence batching — each spends the binding resource (tokens) or violates the artifact/dependency/checkpoint constraints. Realistic target if changes 1-6 land: rating share falls to ~20-30%, tokens-per-MPS improves from 1.7-2.2M toward ~1.0-1.3M within 2-3 rounds, and the suite simultaneously protects the banked 45.4 MPS from regression clawback while pulling verification-conversion credit into the round it is earned.
